title: VPN Kill Switch Explained: Why You Need It and How It Works
description: A VPN kill switch cuts your internet connection if the VPN drops, preventing IP leaks. Here is how it works and why it matters for privacy.
content:
What a Kill Switch Does

A VPN kill switch is a security feature that automatically blocks all internet traffic if your VPN connection drops unexpectedly. Without it, your device falls back to your regular unencrypted connection, exposing your real IP address and traffic to your ISP and anyone else monitoring the network. This can happen in seconds and you may not even notice.

When VPN Connections Drop

VPN connections drop more often than people expect. Switching between Wi-Fi networks, waking a laptop from sleep, server overload on the VPN provider side, and brief network interruptions all trigger disconnections. Each time the connection drops without a kill switch, your real IP leaks. For most casual users this is an inconvenience. For journalists, activists, and people in restrictive environments, it is a serious risk.

System-Level vs Application-Level Kill Switch

A system-level kill switch blocks all internet traffic on the device when the VPN is down. Nothing can connect, not your browser, not background apps, not system processes. An application-level kill switch only blocks specific apps you designate. You might set it so that your torrent client or browser is blocked, but system updates and other apps continue. Most VPN apps offer both options.

How to Enable the Kill Switch

In most VPN apps, the kill switch is in the Settings or Security section. It is often disabled by default. Look for terms like Kill Switch, Network Lock, or Internet Kill Switch depending on the provider. ExpressVPN calls it Network Lock, NordVPN calls it Kill Switch, Mullvad calls it Block internet access when VPN is disconnected. Enable it and test it by disconnecting the VPN manually while checking your IP at a site like whatismyip.com.

Limitations

A kill switch does not protect against DNS leaks, WebRTC leaks, or IPv6 leaks, which are separate vulnerabilities. It also does not help if your VPN provider itself is compromised or logging your data. Use it alongside a no-logs provider and periodic leak tests for complete protection.
